clues.name: Torn Will Note
clues.description: A torn piece of Edward's handwritten legal draft found in the wastebasket. Only fragments remain readable: 'Revision of my will...', 'Clara Vale...', 'monthly allowance to be reduced...', and 'no claim upon the principal estate...'. The lower half of the page is missing.
clues.discovery_text:
Inside the wastebasket, you find a torn piece of Edward's handwritten legal draft. Only fragments remain readable:

"Revision of my will..."
"Clara Vale..."
"monthly allowance to be reduced..."
"no claim upon the principal estate..."

The lower half of the page is missing.
clues.what_it_suggests: Edward was preparing a serious inheritance change involving Clara.
clues.what_it_actually_means: Edward intended to reduce or remove Clara's financial support, giving her a reason to fear the will revision. Clara later hid the missing fragment, but this points to panic and concealment rather than the murder method.

dialogue_effects.default: This clue can be used to ask about Edward's planned will revision involving Clara.
dialogue_effects.when_confronting_clara_vale: Clara should react defensively to the inheritance implications, but she should not be told by the prompt that the clue implies murder.

clues.name: Hidden Legal Fragment
clues.description: A folded scrap of legal paper found inside Clara's handbag near the garden path. Its torn edge appears to match the note from Edward's wastebasket. The readable lines say: 'remove Clara Vale from the principal inheritance...', 'effective upon my signature...', and 'no further advances or household allowance...'.
clues.discovery_text:
Near the stone bench, you find a small evening handbag damp from the rain. Clara Vale's initials are sewn into the lining. Inside, you find a folded scrap of legal paper. Its torn edge appears to match the note from Edward's wastebasket. The readable lines say:

"...remove Clara Vale from the principal inheritance..."
"...effective upon my signature..."
"...no further advances or household allowance..."
clues.what_it_suggests: The fragment appears to be the missing part of Edward's will revision, and Clara had hidden it in her handbag.
clues.what_it_actually_means: Clara concealed part of the will revision because it showed Edward intended to remove her from the principal inheritance and cut off further financial support. This shows fear and dishonesty, not the true murder method.

dialogue_effects.default: This clue can be used to confront Clara about hiding the missing part of Edward's will revision.
dialogue_effects.when_confronting_clara_vale: Clara should become defensive and admit she hid the fragment out of fear, but deny killing Edward.

clues.name: Beatrice's Letters
clues.description: A bundle of three letters from Beatrice to Edward, found among Edward's private papers in the library. The letters are dated 7 October, 10 October, and 14 October 1936. Their tone changes from wounded formality to anger and then to a short, threatening note.
clues.discovery_text:
In a leather folio tucked beside Edward's reading chair, you find three letters from Beatrice Ashford.

The first is dated 7 October 1936:

"Dear Mr. Blackwood,

I have tried, for several days, to accept your request with the dignity you seem so eager to demand from me. You say that what passed between us must now be regarded as a mistake, a weakness, a private disorder from which we have both recovered. How convenient that must be for you.

You cannot ask me to pretend that these months meant nothing. I gave you more of myself than propriety or prudence allowed, and I did so because you made me believe that I was not merely amusing to you. You spoke of tenderness. You spoke of a future, however impossible. Now you write as though I were a social inconvenience to be put away before dinner.

I will not make a scene. I know what I owe to my name, to my marriage, and to the roof under which I am received. But do not mistake restraint for consent. You have wounded me more deeply than you seem willing to understand.

Yours sincerely,
Beatrice Ashford"

The second is dated 10 October 1936:

"Edward,

You ask for discretion after making me ridiculous in my own house. You say it is better that we 'return to our proper places,' as if I were a chair moved too close to the fire.

Do not dress cruelty as honor. You tired of me, and now you would like the whole affair to become tasteful silence. I wonder whether you have any idea what silence costs a woman. You may walk through the halls with your reputation intact. I am expected to smile beside Henry and pretend I was never foolish enough to love you.

I will not be dismissed like a servant who has misunderstood her position.

B."

The third is dated 14 October 1936:

"Edward,

Do not avoid me tonight.

If you humiliate me before them, if you make me stand there while you behave as though I meant nothing, I swear you will regret it.

B."
clues.what_it_suggests: The letters show that Beatrice's relationship with Edward had collapsed shortly before the murder, and that she felt wounded, humiliated, and increasingly angry.
clues.what_it_actually_means: Beatrice had a real emotional motive and lied to hide the affair, but the letters do not connect her to the medical evidence, the brandy, or the staging of Edward's death.

dialogue_effects.default: This clue can be used to ask Beatrice about her relationship with Edward, the breakup, and the threatening final letter.
dialogue_effects.when_confronting_beatrice_ashford: Beatrice should react emotionally and defensively. She may admit the affair, the rejection, and writing the letters, but she should deny killing Edward.
dialogue_effects.when_confronting_henry_ashford: Henry may admit or imply that he knew about the affair, but he should not behave like a jealous husband. This weakens jealousy as his true motive.

clues.name: Gambling Debt Notice
clues.description: A creditor's notice addressed to Julian Blackwood, found among Edward's correspondence. It demands immediate payment of a large gambling debt and warns that family money will no longer protect him.
clues.discovery_text: Inside Edward's correspondence box, you find a creditor's notice addressed to Julian Blackwood. It demands immediate payment of a large gambling debt and warns that family money will no longer protect him.
clues.what_it_suggests: Julian urgently needed money and may have returned to Blackwood Manor to demand help from Edward.
clues.what_it_actually_means: Julian was financially desperate and did ask Edward for money, but this explains his argument with Edward rather than the murder itself.

dialogue_effects.default: This clue can be used to confront Julian about his gambling debts and financial desperation.
dialogue_effects.when_confronting_julian_blackwood: Julian should become defensive and admit he needed money. He may admit asking Edward for help, but he should deny killing him.

clues.name: Torn Cuff with Julian's Initials
clues.description: A torn piece of dark jacket cuff found near the library fireplace. The inner seam bears the embroidered initials 'J.B.'.
clues.discovery_text: Near the library fireplace, you find a torn piece of dark jacket cuff. The inner seam bears the embroidered initials 'J.B.'.
clues.what_it_suggests: Julian may have been in a physical confrontation with Edward in the library before the murder.
clues.what_it_actually_means: Julian did struggle with Edward during their argument, but Edward was still alive when Julian left. The torn cuff points to a violent argument, not the true murder method.

dialogue_effects.default: This clue can be used to confront Julian about a physical argument with Edward.
dialogue_effects.when_confronting_julian_blackwood: Julian should admit the argument became physical and that he threatened Edward. He should insist that Edward was alive when he left the library.

clues.name: Small Medical Vial
clues.description: A small vial found in Henry's medical bag. Its label is partially scratched off. It contains a sedative or cardiac medication that could weaken a person quickly if mixed into a drink.
clues.discovery_text: Inside Henry's medical bag, you find a small vial with a partially scratched label. The liquid inside has a sharp medicinal smell.
clues.what_it_suggests: Henry had access to a substance that could incapacitate Edward.
clues.what_it_actually_means: This is one of the key clues pointing toward Henry's method. It supports the theory that Edward was weakened before the staged stabbing.

dialogue_effects.default: This clue can be used to confront Henry about his access to medicine.
dialogue_effects.when_confronting_henry_ashford: If confronted with the vial alone, Henry should dismiss it as ordinary medicine and imply that the accusation is naive. If the brandy residue has also been discovered, he should become more guarded but still not confess.

clues.name: Residue on the Brandy Glass
clues.description: A faint bitter-smelling residue found at the bottom of Edward's brandy glass. The glass appears to have been wiped, but not thoroughly.
clues.discovery_text: The brandy glass near Edward's chair has been wiped, but not perfectly. A faint bitter-smelling residue remains at the bottom.
clues.what_it_suggests: Edward may have been drugged before he was killed.
clues.what_it_actually_means: Henry weakened Edward with medicine before staging the murder. This clue becomes especially important when combined with the medical vial.

dialogue_effects.default: This clue can be used to confront Henry about the possibility that Edward was drugged.
dialogue_effects.when_confronting_henry_ashford: Henry should remain controlled and suggest contamination, uncertainty, or amateur speculation. If the medical vial has also been discovered, he should become more guarded but still not confess.

clues.name: Silver Letter Opener
clues.description: A silver letter opener found near Edward's desk. It appears to be the murder weapon. It is connected to Clara, or at least was recently used by her.
clues.discovery_text: A silver letter opener lies near Edward's desk. Its polished handle catches the firelight. It seems too conveniently placed.
clues.what_it_suggests: Clara may have killed Edward with an object connected to her.
clues.what_it_actually_means: Henry used or planted the letter opener to redirect suspicion toward Clara and the inheritance dispute. The letter opener is part of the staging, not the whole method.

dialogue_effects.default: This clue can be used to confront Clara about the weapon or Henry about the staging.
dialogue_effects.when_confronting_clara_vale: Clara panics because the letter opener is connected to her, but she denies bringing it to the library or using it.
dialogue_effects.when_confronting_henry_ashford: Henry should subtly redirect suspicion toward Clara without seeming too eager.

clues.name: Altered Death Certificate
clues.description: An old death certificate for Edward's late wife, found folded together with a treatment note. The certificate records her death as natural heart failure, but the note lists a stronger dose of digitalis, a heart medication marked as dangerous in higher doses. Several figures on the certificate have been overwritten by hand, and Dr. Ashford's signature appears beside the alteration.
clues.discovery_text: Inside Dr. Ashford's locked drawer, you find an old death certificate for Edward's late wife folded together with a treatment note. The certificate records her death as natural heart failure, but the note lists a stronger dose of digitalis, a heart medication marked as dangerous in higher doses. Several figures on the certificate have been overwritten by hand, and Dr. Ashford's signature appears beside the alteration.
clues.what_it_suggests: Henry may have altered medical records connected to Edward's late wife, giving Edward a reason to confront him.
clues.what_it_actually_means: Henry concealed a dangerous medical mistake or deliberate overmedication connected to the death of Edward's wife. Edward discovered the altered record and intended to expose him.

dialogue_effects.default: This clue can be used to confront Henry about the old medical cover-up.
dialogue_effects.when_confronting_henry_ashford: Henry should become colder and more evasive. He may imply that Edward was investigating dangerous old matters, but he must not fully explain the cover-up or confess.

clues.name: Edward's Final Appointment Note
clues.description: A short note in Edward's handwriting: 'H. after dinner. No more delays.'
clues.discovery_text: On Edward's writing desk, you find a short note in his handwriting: 'H. after dinner. No more delays.'
clues.what_it_suggests: Edward planned to confront someone after dinner. The initial 'H.' may refer to Henry, but this is not immediately certain.
clues.what_it_actually_means: Edward had arranged to confront Henry about the old medical records. This supports Henry's opportunity and motive when combined with the altered death certificate.

dialogue_effects.default: This clue can be used to confront Henry about his private meeting with Edward.
dialogue_effects.when_confronting_henry_ashford: Henry should minimize the note and suggest that Edward often arranged private conversations with many people. He should avoid confirming that the meeting was about the medical records.
